title: Putting the ram in for tupping
subtitle: Timing the introduction and managing the tupping period so ewes lamb in the window that suits the holding.
excerpt: Working backwards from the desired lambing date gives the introduction date: the ewe's gestation is 147 days on average, and the ram needs five weeks with the flock to cover a full two oestrus cycles. Get the timing wrong and ewes lamb at the wrong season. This covers the timing calculation, harness-and-crayon marking, and what to watch during the tupping period.
sourceNotes:
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Better Returns Programme: Improving Lambing Percentage (2013). https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk

content.text: starts with the lambing window you want. Ewes carry lambs for 147 days on average, so count back from your target first-lamb date to find the introduction date. In the UK, most breeds go into oestrus from October onward as days shorten. Bringing the ram in late October gives April lambing; early November gives late April to May.

two to three weeks before the ram goes in. Move ewes onto the best available grazing or supplement lightly with concentrate. Condition score: ewes at 2.5 to 3.5 on the 1-to-5 scale have the best lambing rates. Trim hooves if they are overgrown. Check feet for foot rot: a lame ewe at tupping loses condition quickly and may miss oestrus cycles.

crayon on the ram's brisket marks each ewe he serves: she carries a coloured smear on her rump. Start with a light colour (yellow or white). After 16 days, change to a darker colour (red or blue). Ewes marked with the second colour were not served in the first cycle and are coming back into oestrus, which may indicate the ram is not working efficiently or the ewe has a fertility issue.

Check the flock once a day. Confirm the ram is active and not limping. A lame ram may stop serving. Count the marks daily: by day 10 to 14 most ewes should show marks if the timing and ram fertility are right. A flock where only a few ewes are marked by day 16 warrants a fertility check on the ram before assuming ewe problems.

Remove the ram after five weeks. This gives two full oestrus cycles plus a few days' margin. A ram left in longer than five to six weeks produces a very spread-out lambing and makes the scanning-to-lambing window calculation less reliable. Record the removal date and calculate the expected scanning date (around day 70 of gestation) and the lambing window (days 140 to 155 from introduction) before the farm diary closes on the year.
